content:
To solve this problem, we need to place dominoes on a $1000 \times 1000$ board such that no two dominoes are adjacent but they can touch at vertices. We will start by analyzing how to maximize the number of non-adjacent dominoes.

Firstly, consider coloring the $1000 \times 1000$ board in a checkerboard pattern where each cell is either black or white. In such a pattern, no two adjacent cells have the same color, and there are an equal number of black and white cells (since $1000 \times 1000 = 1000000$ which is even).

A domino covers exactly one black cell and one white cell. Since we cannot place two dominoes adjacent to each other, the most efficient way to maximize the number of dominoes is to use a pattern where every second row or column is skipped.

Let's consider placing dominoes only on the black cells in every other row (i.e., rows 1, 3, 5, ..., 999). This means we will place dominoes in half of the rows. In each of these rows, since there are no adjacency constraints within a row and between adjacent columns (only vertical adjacency), we can place as many dominoes as possible without violating the adjacency rule.

Each row has $1000$ cells, so in every selected row (row 1, 3, 5, ..., 999), we have $500$ black and $500$ white cells. We can place $500$ dominoes per such row because each domino covers one black and one white cell.

Since there are 500 such rows (rows 1, 3, 5, ..., 999), the total number of dominoes that can be placed is:
\[ 500 \text{ dominoes/row} \times 500 \text{ rows} = 250000. \]

Therefore, the maximum number of dominoes that can be placed on the board under the given conditions is \(\boxed{250000}\).
